intro:
The doctrine of Christ's threefold office — prophet, priest, and king (munus triplex) — organizes his saving work under three categories corresponding to Israel's three anointed roles. As prophet, Christ revealed God's will perfectly and definitively: his teaching carried the authority of divine self-disclosure ('You have heard... but I say to you'), and he is the Word made flesh in whom all divine revelation is embodied (Heb. 1:1-2). Moses had promised a greater prophet to come (Deut. 18:15-18), and Jesus fulfilled this role by speaking as one having authority (Matt. 7:29), by confirming his words through signs, and by being himself the fullness of divine communication. The prophetic office encompasses both his earthly teaching ministry and his ongoing instruction of the church through the Spirit.

As priest, Christ offered the definitive sacrifice — himself — once for all, accomplishing what the Levitical system anticipated without achieving: 'he entered the Most Holy Place once for all by his own blood, thus obtaining eternal redemption' (Heb. 9:12). His ongoing high-priestly work includes perpetual intercession for believers (Heb. 7:25; Rom. 8:34). As king, Christ was designated heir of David's throne (Luke 1:32-33; 2 Sam. 7:13-14) and exercises lordship over all creation and the church (Eph. 1:20-23; Rev. 19:16). The Psalter's royal psalms (Ps. 2; 45; 72; 110) are consistently applied to Christ throughout the NT. Developed especially by John Calvin in the Institutes (2.15), the threefold office schema provides a comprehensive synthetic account of how Christ completes Israel's covenant institutions while transcending them.
